focus_areas: C extension memory safety (ext/nokogiri/*.c, libxml2 boundary)
Entity handling / XXE / parse-option enforcement (libxml2_polyfill.c, parse_options.rb, schema/relaxng/dtd)
Ruby API layer (lib/nokogiri/**.rb, encoding sniffing, dynamic dispatch)

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read in required_attributes: loop bounded by attrs_depr but indexes attrs_req
findings.description: In required_attributes() the NULL check is on description->attrs_req (line 27), but the loop termination condition reads description->attrs_depr[i] (line 29) while the body dereferences description->attrs_req[i] (line 30). attrs_req and attrs_depr are two independent NULL-terminated arrays in libxml2's static htmlElemDesc tables and frequently have different lengths. If attrs_depr is longer than attrs_req, the loop runs past attrs_req's NULL terminator, reading attrs_req[i] out of bounds and passing it to NOKOGIRI_STR_NEW2 -> strlen() on a garbage pointer. The sibling functions deprecated_attributes/optional_attributes/sub_elements correctly index the same array they iterate, confirming this is a copy-paste defect. The descriptor is selected via htmlTagLookup(StringValueCStr(tag_name)); elements such as deprecated applet/basefont/isindex carry a longer attrs_depr than a short non-NULL attrs_req, making the OOB reachable.
findings.exploit_scenario: Ruby code calls Nokogiri::HTML4::ElementDescription[tag].required_attributes for an element whose attrs_depr is longer than its non-NULL attrs_req. The loop iterates by attrs_depr's length, dereferences attrs_req[i] past its terminator, performs an OOB read of adjacent rodata pointers and calls strlen() on an out-of-bounds value, potentially crashing or leaking adjacent static memory into the returned String.
findings.recommendation: Change the loop condition to iterate the same array being indexed: for (i = 0; description->attrs_req[i]; i++).
findings.confidence_reason: Confirmed copy-paste defect (loop bounds on attrs_depr, indexes attrs_req); libxml2 tables do contain elements where attrs_depr outlives a non-NULL attrs_req, so the OOB read is reachable, though impact is bounded to static-memory adjacency.

findings.title: CSS #id selector emits unescaped, escape-decoded id value into a single-quoted XPath literal
findings.description: visit_id builds "@id='#{Regexp.last_match(1)}'" from the id value with no quoting/escaping. The id value is produced by unescape_css_identifier (lib/nokogiri/css/parser.rb), which decodes CSS escape sequences including numeric escapes: \27 decodes to U+0027 (apostrophe). The HASH token regex admits {escape} sequences, so an attacker-controlled selector segment like #x\27 ... yields an id value containing a literal apostrophe that closes the XPath string literal early, letting the remainder be parsed as XPath expression context. Flow: untrusted selector -> doc.css(...)/Nokogiri::CSS.xpath_for -> Parser -> :ID node value = unescape_css_identifier -> visit_id raw interpolation. The attribute-equality path (visit_attribute_condition) defends embedded quotes with a concat() rewrite; visit_id has none. Verified end-to-end: unescape happens before visit_id and the apostrophe is not re-escaped downstream.
findings.exploit_scenario: An application builds a CSS query from user input for a filtering/ACL decision, e.g. doc.css("##{params[:id]}"). An attacker supplies an id like x\27 or\20 \271\27 =\271 (escapes decode to x' or '1'='1'), yielding XPath //*[@id='x' or '1'='1'], matching nodes the application intended to exclude. The CSS-escape vector also bypasses naive app-side filters that block raw quote characters, since the quote only materializes after CSS decoding.
findings.recommendation: Emit the id value as a properly escaped/quoted XPath string literal via a shared xpath_quote(value) helper that handles embedded single and double quotes (reuse the concat()-based quoting from the attribute path), applied in visit_id, visit_class_condition, and css_class.
findings.confidence_reason: Verified: HASH token admits \27 escape, unescape_css_identifier decodes it to apostrophe before visit_id, and visit_id raw-interpolates with no concat() defense, yielding a genuine single-quoted XPath literal breakout; minor deduction since exploitation requires app-side interpolation into doc.css.

findings.title: CSS class selector / ~= includes value interpolated unescaped into XPath string literal in css_class
findings.description: css_class(hay, needle) interpolates needle directly into XPath string literals with no escaping: the builtin branch produces "nokogiri-builtin:css-class(#{hay},'#{needle}')" (line 368) and the default branch "contains(concat(' ',normalize-space(#{hay}),' '),' #{needle} ')" (line 371). needle originates from visit_class_condition (CLASS_CONDITION value = unescape_css_identifier) and the attribute ~= :includes branch. unescape_css_identifier decodes \27 to an apostrophe, so a selector like .foo\27 bar decodes to needle foo'bar, producing ...'foo'bar...' which closes the XPath literal early. Unlike the attribute-equality path, css_class has no concat() protection. doc.css uses OPTIMAL, hitting the builtin branch (line 368) on the libxml backend; the else branch is equally vulnerable on other backends.
findings.exploit_scenario: An app does doc.css(".tag-#{user_tag}") or doc.css("[class~='#{user_tag}']") to scope which content a user may see. The attacker supplies a tag value containing a \27 escape so the decoded needle breaks out of the ' ... ' literal and alters which nodes the contains(...) predicate matches, selecting nodes outside the intended class filter.
findings.recommendation: Escape needle (and hay if ever attacker-influenced) before interpolation in css_class using a shared XPath string-literal quoting helper that handles embedded ' and ". Also fix the :includes branch so quote-stripping happens on the raw decoded value before any concat rewrite, then re-quote safely.
findings.confidence_reason: Verified end-to-end: \27 unescapes to apostrophe, needle interpolated unquoted into the XPath literal in both css_class branches with no concat protection, and doc.css uses OPTIMAL hitting the builtin branch on libxml; real XPath injection gated only by an app interpolating untrusted input into a class selector.

findings.title: NULL-pointer dereference in push-parser free when context never initialized
findings.description: xml_sax_push_parser_free dereferences ctx->myDoc (line 9) before the if(ctx) NULL check (line 12). allocate wraps a NULL data pointer (line 28) and DATA_PTR is only set after xmlCreatePushParserCtxt succeeds (line 99). If a PushParser is allocated but garbage-collected without a successful initialize_native (e.g. .allocate called directly, or init raises between allocate and the DATA_PTR assignment - xmlCreatePushParserCtxt returning NULL, or StringValueCStr/unwrap raising on bad args), ctx is NULL and ctx->myDoc dereferences NULL during GC. The same free function is shared by the HTML4 push parser. Reachable via Ruby-API misuse / init-failure rather than untrusted document bytes (document content is fed later via write).
findings.exploit_scenario: obj = Nokogiri::XML::SAX::PushParser.allocate followed by letting obj be garbage collected (never calling the Ruby initializer), or an initialize that raises before line 99. During sweep, xml_sax_push_parser_free(NULL) executes NULL->myDoc, crashing the process.
findings.recommendation: Reorder so the NULL check precedes the dereference: add if (ctx == NULL) { return; } at the top of xml_sax_push_parser_free before accessing ctx->myDoc.
findings.confidence_reason: Real NULL-deref (ctx->myDoc before if(ctx)) confirmed, but only reachable via Ruby-API misuse/init-failure, not untrusted document bytes - genuine but low-severity, low-exploitability for a library trust boundary.

findings.title: Transform passes unchecked NULL result document to wrap, causing NULL-pointer dereference
findings.description: In rb_xslt_stylesheet_transform, c_result_document = xsltApplyStylesheet(wrapper->ss, c_document, params) (line 336) is never checked for NULL. The only failure guard is the generic-error-string check at line 347. xsltApplyStylesheet can return NULL on failures that do not route through xsltSetGenericErrorFunc (allocation failures, certain transform-context init failures, silent early-returns on invalid stylesheet/document combinations), leaving rb_error_str empty. Execution then falls through to noko_xml_document_wrap((VALUE)0, c_result_document) with c_result_document == NULL, reaching _xml_document_data_ptr_set (xml_document.c). assert(c_document->_private == NULL) dereferences NULL but is compiled out under NDEBUG (release gems), so the subsequent c_document->_private = tuple writes through the NULL pointer, crashing the process.
findings.exploit_scenario: A transform whose stylesheet/input combination causes xsltApplyStylesheet to return NULL without populating the generic error string (e.g. an allocation failure or a silent libxslt early-return) reaches noko_xml_document_wrap with NULL; _xml_document_data_ptr_set writes tuple through NULL->_private, segfaulting the Ruby process (DoS) rather than raising a recoverable exception. The most-cited terminate="yes" case is likely caught by the hooked generic-error handler.
findings.recommendation: After line 336, check for a NULL result before wrapping: if c_result_document is NULL and no parse error was recorded, raise a RuntimeError ("could not perform xslt transform on document"); only fall back to the error-string path when a result exists. Do this after restoring error handlers and freeing params.
findings.confidence_reason: NULL result write-through is real and unguarded (assert compiled out under NDEBUG); xsltApplyStylesheet can return NULL with an empty error string via OOM/silent early-returns, though the cited terminate="yes" case likely routes through the hooked generic-error handler.

findings.title: canonicalize leaks c_namespaces and output buffer when a namespace string contains an embedded NUL
findings.description: In rb_xml_document_canonicalize, the inclusive_namespaces array is marshalled at lines 648-652: c_namespaces = ruby_xcalloc(...) then a loop calling c_namespaces[j] = StringValueCStr(entry). StringValueCStr raises ArgumentError if the Ruby string contains an embedded NUL. That raise propagates before line 661 (ruby_xfree(c_namespaces)) and line 662 (xmlOutputBufferClose(c_obuf)), leaking both the c_namespaces allocation and the libxml2 output buffer allocated at line 633. This is the same leak class fixed for the XSLT transform path (GHSA-v2fc-qm4h-8hqv, remediated with rb_protect), but the fix was not applied to the C14N namespace-marshalling loop. inclusive_namespaces is caller-supplied; each call with a NUL-containing entry leaks.
findings.exploit_scenario: Code calls doc.canonicalize(mode, ["ns\x00evil"]) where the inclusive-namespace list derives from attacker-influenced input. StringValueCStr raises on the embedded NUL; if the caller rescues and retries, c_namespaces and the xmlOutputBuffer leak each iteration, and sustained calls exhaust memory.
findings.recommendation: Marshal the namespaces under rb_protect (mirroring build_xslt_params), or restructure so ruby_xfree(c_namespaces) and xmlOutputBufferClose(c_obuf) run on every exit path (e.g. validate the array for NUL-free strings before allocating, or use rb_ensure).
findings.confidence_reason: Code confirms xcalloc + StringValueCStr loop with no rb_protect/rb_ensure, so an embedded-NUL raise leaks c_namespaces and c_obuf; real but low-impact per-call leak requiring caller-supplied NUL.

findings.title: Quadratic backtracking in JRuby meta-charset encoding-detection regex on full untrusted document
findings.description: EncodingReader.detect_encoding(chunk) runs, on the JRuby branch (Nokogiri.jruby?, line 63), the regex /(<meta\s)(.*)(charset\s*=\s*([\w-]+))(.*)/i against chunk (line 64). The pattern contains two greedy .* groups separated only by the required literal charset\s*=\s*[\w-]+. For input beginning with <meta but containing no charset= token, the engine lets the first .* consume to end of line, then backtracks one position at a time re-attempting the literal, producing O(n^2) work per physical line. In the string (memory) parse path the ENTIRE document is passed unbounded: HTML4::Document.parse(input) -> encoding ||= EncodingReader.detect_encoding(input) (html4/document.rb ~228), reached via Nokogiri.HTML(str)/HTML4.parse(str). The IO path reads only ~1KB chunks and is bounded. CRuby uses a SAX push parser and does not hit this regex; the issue is JRuby-only and quadratic (not exponential).
findings.exploit_scenario: On a JRuby deployment, an application parses user-supplied HTML via Nokogiri.HTML(body) without specifying an encoding (common for scraping/sanitizing). The attacker submits a single long newline-free line such as "<meta " + ("a" * 2_000_000) with no charset= substring. detect_encoding evaluates the line-64 regex against the whole string; the two greedy .* groups force ~O(n^2) backtracking, pinning a CPU core; repeated requests amplify into denial of service.
findings.recommendation: Bound and de-backtrack the regex: only run detection on a bounded prefix (e.g. chunk[0,1024], matching the IO/CRuby budget) and rewrite the pattern to avoid two unbounded greedy .* around a literal, e.g. /<meta\b[^>]*?charset\s*=\s*([\w-]+)/i. Apply consistently to both memory and IO branches.
findings.confidence_reason: Regex shape, JRuby-only gating, and full-input string path all verified; quadratic backtracking is real but requires a literal <meta plus a long newline-free run, is only quadratic (not exponential), and JRuby-only - a genuine but moderate ReDoS.

findings.title: Unchecked xmlAllocOutputBuffer return dereferenced in canonicalize
findings.description: At line 633, c_obuf = xmlAllocOutputBuffer(NULL) is not checked for NULL. Lines 635-637 immediately dereference it (c_obuf->writecallback, c_obuf->closecallback, c_obuf->context). xmlAllocOutputBuffer returns NULL on allocation failure; under memory pressure this is a NULL-pointer write. Low severity/confidence because it requires OOM with no attacker control, but it is a genuine unchecked-return dereference on the C14N path.
findings.exploit_scenario: Under memory exhaustion, xmlAllocOutputBuffer returns NULL; line 635 writes through the NULL pointer, crashing the process rather than raising a Ruby NoMemoryError. No attacker control over the trigger beyond inducing global memory exhaustion.
findings.recommendation: Check if (c_obuf == NULL) { rb_raise(rb_eNoMemError, "could not allocate output buffer"); } immediately after line 633.
findings.confidence_reason: Real latent NULL-deref but only triggers under OOM with no attacker control; defense-in-depth robustness issue, not actionable as a security vuln.

findings.title: size_t caret-diagnostic length truncated to int in rb_utf8_str_new
findings.description: In add_errors(), gumbo_caret_diagnostic_to_string() returns a size_t length (line 318) passed to rb_utf8_str_new(msg, (int)size) at line 319. The diagnostic length is driven by error->position.column, which drives a memset of column-1 bytes in caret_diagnostic_to_string (error.c). For a single input line exceeding ~2 GiB the diagnostic exceeds INT_MAX and (int)size becomes negative/truncated before being handed to rb_utf8_str_new. This is primarily a volumetric/oversized-input concern (the >2 GiB line is the cost), flagged because it is a genuine size_t->int sign-truncation at a trust boundary; no clean small-input trigger was found and the value feeds a copy of an already-allocated buffer.
findings.exploit_scenario: An attacker supplies an HTML document with one line of ~2 GiB of non-newline bytes that triggers a parse error on that line. The caret diagnostic is >INT_MAX bytes; the truncated/negative int length passed to rb_utf8_str_new could mis-size the Ruby string. Requires multi-GiB input, so impact is bounded by the volumetric precondition.
findings.recommendation: Clamp the diagnostic length before the cast (cap size to INT_MAX or a sane diagnostic-line limit) and use a size-checked Ruby string constructor; alternatively bound column-derived output in caret_diagnostic_to_string().
findings.confidence_reason: Real (int) sign-truncation cast at a trust boundary, but reaching INT_MAX requires a >2GiB single input line - a volumetric precondition the exclusion rules treat as a DoS FP - and the truncated value feeds a copy of an already-allocated buffer, so no realistic OOB beyond the volumetric trigger.

findings.title: find_prev/next_newline rely on assert-only bounds for error_location within source buffer
findings.description: find_prev_newline() (lines 387-401) and find_next_newline() (lines 406-418) scan from error_location with only assert(error_location >= source_text) / assert(error_location <= source_end) guarding bounds. Under NDEBUG (release gems) these asserts compile out, and the while-loops would walk off the buffer if any GumboError were recorded with original_text.data not pointing into the buffer passed to gumbo_caret_diagnostic_to_string. All reviewed tokenizer error sites set original_text.data from utf8iterator pointers into the same input, so the invariant currently holds and no concrete violating path was found; reported as a defense-in-depth gap.
findings.exploit_scenario: If any current or future error-generation path (or a desync between the buffer used for parsing and the buffer passed to the diagnostic API) yields an original_text.data outside [source_text, source_end], the newline scan reads out of bounds while building a syntax-error message. No such path is presently reachable from the Ruby API.
findings.recommendation: Replace the asserts with runtime clamps: if error_location is outside [source_text, source_end], return source_text / source_end respectively instead of scanning.
findings.confidence_reason: Asserts are the only bound and compile out under NDEBUG, but no reachable path produces an out-of-buffer error_location - pure defense-in-depth hardening gap with no concrete exploit (exclusion rule 13).

findings.title: :contains() argument passed through with limited but possible unsafe value sources
findings.description: visit_function emits "contains(.,#{node.value[1]})" for :contains() (lines 138-139). For a STRING argument the value retains its source quotes from the tokenizer and the string1/string2 patterns forbid an unescaped matching quote, so direct breakout is hard. The expr grammar also accepts IDENT and xpath_attribute arguments passed without unescape and without quoting, but the tokenizer nmchar character class excludes parens, quotes, and spaces, so no metacharacter reaches the interpolation. No concrete reachable breakout was found; reported as same-class hardening.
findings.exploit_scenario: If an application built a selector string with user text inside a :contains(...) argument that is later re-parsed, the unquoted/unnormalized argument handling could in principle alter predicate semantics. Lower confidence: the STRING path resists single-quote breakout and IDENT/attr paths cannot carry injection metacharacters.
findings.recommendation: Normalize and re-quote the :contains() argument through the same shared XPath string-literal quoting helper recommended for F-002/F-003 rather than passing token text through verbatim.
findings.confidence_reason: Tokenizer char classes (string excludes unescaped quotes; IDENT/nmchar excludes parens/quotes/spaces) block every interpolation path into contains(.,...), and no concrete breakout is demonstrated or constructible.
